title: Narrow Pantry Shelving
summary: Wall-cleat pantry shelving that wraps the closet: 1x3 supports screwed to studs, solid shelves on top, slim 1x3 legs at the front. No brackets, no sag, no wasted inch.
description:
This is the shelving method closet systems don't want you to know about: 1x3 cleats screwed level into the studs, shelf boards laid on the cleats, and slender 1x3 legs at the front edges where spans get long. It wraps three walls of a pantry, costs a fraction of wire systems, and holds canned goods — the heaviest thing most houses store — without a single bracket.

There's no cut list because your closet IS the cut list: every cleat and shelf cuts about 1/4" shy of the wall-to-wall measurement so pieces actually fit into the space (a shelf cut exact binds on drywall texture and paint). The material flexes too — 1x12s for narrow shelves, 15-3/4" panels for deep ones, whatever the closet wants.

The genuinely clever bit is the screw-pattern trick in step 3: studs run vertically, so once the bottom cleat's screws found the studs, you START screws in the next cleat directly above those, then lift it into place and drive — every subsequent cleat self-locates the studs. And the front-leg rules at the end (plumb, cut to the top shelf, one screw per shelf, legs about every 36" of span) are what keep a loaded 3/4" shelf flat for decades.

tools.note: Every cleat gets leveled, every leg gets plumbed. This build is 80% level work.
Marking stud centers — the map the whole project runs on.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Cleats and front legs — buy linear feet equal to your shelving run plus legs. Furring strips work at half the price.

materials.name: 1x12 or shelving panel, 8 ft
materials.note: The shelves. 15-3/4" laminated panels for deeper shelves; scale quantity to your closet.

materials.name: Cabinet screws, #8 x 1-1/4"
materials.note: Shelves to cleats, legs to shelves.

steps.title: Find and mark the studs
steps.body:
Run a stud finder across each wall at roughly bottom-shelf height and mark every stud — aim for the CENTER (studs are 1-1/2" wide; a screw at the edge finds air).

These marks are the project's foundation. A cleat screwed to drywall alone holds a cleat's weight, briefly.

steps.title: Install the bottom cleat, dead level
steps.body: Cut a 1x3 about 1/4" less than the wall's width and screw it to the studs with 2-1/2" cabinet screws, one per stud, holding it against the level as you drive. That 1/4" shortfall is deliberate: exact-cut boards fight the walls and lose.

steps.title: Clone the screw pattern up the wall
steps.body:
The trick that makes the rest fast: lay the next cleat on top of the installed one and START screws directly above the existing screw locations — not driven through, just bitten in. Lift that cleat to the next shelf height, level it, and drive the pre-placed screws home. They're already aimed at studs.

Repeat for every shelf level on that wall.

steps.title: Wrap the side walls
steps.body: Same method around the closet's side walls. Where a corner leaves no stud in reach, the plan's fix applies: drive screws at an angle into the corner or header framing — there's always wood in a corner if you angle toward it.

steps.title: Lay the main shelves
steps.body: Cut each shelf about 1/4" less than the inside width, set it on its cleats — bottom shelf first, working up so you're never reaching over finished work — and screw down into the cleats with 1-1/4" screws.

steps.title: Join wrapped corners
steps.body: Where shelves meet at an inside corner, join the two boards — a 2-1/2" screw driven at an angle, or a pocket screw if you've got the jig. The joint keeps the corner from developing a step as boards season.

steps.title: Add the front legs
steps.body:
The front edge of a long shelf run needs legs — one per ~36" of span for 3/4" shelving. For each: cut a 1x3 to reach the TOP shelf, stand it plumb with the level, confirm it bears fully on the floor, and fasten it to the front edge of EVERY shelf it passes with a 1-1/4" screw — checking each shelf is level front-to-back as you pin it.

The legs look too slight to matter. They're in pure compression; they'd hold a truck. Load the pantry and enjoy shelves that will never bow.

images.alt: Organized pantry closet with wrap-around wooden shelving on wall cleats
